era_context: The early 18th century, in the final year of Peter the Great's reign in Russia and the second year of the Yongzheng Emperor's rule in Qing China. The War of Spanish Succession had ended in 1713, and Europe was in a period of relative peace, though with significant diplomatic realignment. The Ottoman Empire remained a major power, and European colonial expansion was accelerating. This was a transitional year in Russian history, as Peter the Great's death would occur in early 1725 (late January in the Julian calendar, early February in the Gregorian calendar).

events.title: Death of Peter the Great; succession crisis in Russia
events.description: Peter the Great died on 28 January 1725 (in the Julian calendar used in Russia at the time; 8 February 1726 in the proleptic Gregorian calendar). His death marked the end of an era of aggressive military expansion and institutional reform. Peter had not clearly designated a successor, leading to a succession crisis. His widow, Catherine (formerly Martha Skavronskaya), was elevated to the throne with the support of the military and the court faction led by Alexander Menshikov. Peter's death was the result of a urinary tract infection and related complications. His reign had transformed Russia from a relatively peripheral European power into a major military and political force.
key_figures: Peter the Great (Tsar and Emperor, r. 1682–1725)

sources.name: Contemporary accounts by foreign diplomats in St. Petersburg
Russian court records and state documents

events.certainty_note: Peter's death date is well-documented in contemporary records. The Julian/Gregorian calendar conversion is certain. The succession crisis is confirmed by multiple sources.

events.title: Founding of the Russian Academy of Sciences
events.description: The Russian Academy of Sciences was formally established in 1725, though the process had begun earlier under Peter the Great's direction. The Academy was founded in St. Petersburg as an institution to promote scientific research and learning in Russia, modeled partly on Western European academies. The Academy's charter was signed by Catherine I shortly after Peter's death, fulfilling one of Peter's final wishes. The institution would become central to Russian intellectual life and scientific advancement. The Academy initially recruited foreign scholars, particularly from Germany, and focused on mathematics, physics, astronomy, and natural history. This was a major institutional achievement that reflected Peter's commitment to modernizing Russia.

sources.name: Charter of the Russian Academy of Sciences (1725)
Russian state records and Academy archives

events.certainty_note: The founding date and charter are well-documented. The Academy's establishment is confirmed in multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: Treaty of Vienna signed between Austria and Russia
events.description: In April 1725, Austria and Russia signed the Treaty of Vienna, a defensive alliance against the Ottoman Empire and other potential threats. The treaty was negotiated in the context of Peter the Great's final months and was intended to formalize Russian-Austrian cooperation. However, the treaty was short-lived and did not prevent the subsequent Russo-Turkish War (1735–1739). The treaty represented an attempt by both powers to create a stable European order following the War of Spanish Succession, but it was undermined by conflicting interests and the rise of new diplomatic alignments. The treaty is significant as one of the last major diplomatic acts of Peter the Great's reign.
key_figures: Peter the Great (Russian signatory, deceased January 1725)
Charles VI (Holy Roman Emperor and Austrian ruler)
events.certainty_note: The treaty date and terms are well-documented in diplomatic archives.

events.title: Vitus Bering departs St. Petersburg for Second Kamchatka Expedition
events.description: Vitus Bering, a Danish navigator in Russian service, departed from St. Petersburg in 1725 to lead the Second Kamchatka Expedition (also called the Great Northern Expedition). The expedition was sponsored by Peter the Great and was intended to explore the northeastern reaches of the Russian Empire and determine the relationship between Asia and North America. Bering's expedition would take several years to reach Kamchatka and begin its explorations. The expedition was one of the most ambitious geographical undertakings of the early 18th century and would result in the first detailed maps of the Russian Far East and the northern Pacific.
key_figures: Peter the Great (expedition sponsor, deceased January 1725)

sources.name: Expedition orders and correspondence from the Russian government

events.certainty_note: The departure date and expedition details are well-documented in Russian state records.

events.title: Yongzheng Emperor continues administrative reforms in Qing China
events.description: In 1725, the Yongzheng Emperor (r. 1722–1735) continued his aggressive program of administrative reform and centralization in Qing China. He expanded the Grand Council (Junjichu), a body of trusted advisors that reported directly to the emperor, thereby reducing the power of the traditional imperial bureaucracy and the Six Ministries. The Yongzheng Emperor also initiated a campaign to suppress heterodox religious sects and to strengthen Confucian orthodoxy. These reforms were part of a broader effort to consolidate imperial power and to prevent the kind of factional disputes that had plagued earlier reigns. The reforms were controversial and generated resentment among the literati, but they were ultimately successful in strengthening imperial authority.

sources.name: Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty (Qing Shilu)

events.certainty_note: The Yongzheng Emperor's administrative reforms are well-documented in Qing state records and later historiography.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Treaty of Vienna (1725-003) has been reassessed by modern historians. Some scholars argue that it was less consequential than once believed, as both Austria and Russia continued to pursue independent foreign policies and the treaty did not prevent subsequent conflicts. The treaty is sometimes presented in older historiography as a major diplomatic achievement, but modern scholarship suggests it was more of a temporary alignment of interests that quickly unraveled. Additionally, the extent to which Peter the Great's death (1725-001) was caused by a urinary tract infection has been debated; some historians have suggested other causes, including poisoning, though the medical evidence supports the infection hypothesis.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1725 is rich, particularly for European and East Asian events. Russian court records and diplomatic correspondence are abundant, and Chinese imperial records are comprehensive. However, the record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military history. The perspectives of common people, merchants, and non-elite populations are underrepresented. The Russian sources reflect the perspective of the centralizing state and may downplay internal dissent or administrative failures. Chinese sources are official and may similarly downplay internal problems or dissent. Indigenous perspectives on Russian exploration are almost entirely absent.

graph_edges.note: Peter the Great's death in January 1725 led directly to Catherine I's assumption of power in 1726
The founding of the Russian Academy of Sciences in 1725 led to the sponsorship of Bering's expedition
The Treaty of Vienna (1725) was undermined by subsequent diplomatic realignments that led to the Russo-Turkish War
Bering's departure in 1725 was the beginning of the Second Kamchatka Expedition that continued through 1726
Yongzheng's administrative reforms in 1725 were part of his broader program of centralization (1722–1735)
